threats.title: SurplusGuildMinter Slashing Bug via Uninitialized Memory
threats.hypothesis_description: The `getRewards` function checks if a user should be slashed using an uninitialized memory variable, causing any user to be slashed if the term has ever experienced a loss.
threats.investigation_rationale: In `getRewards`, `slashed` is evaluated as `lastGaugeLoss > uint256(userStake.lastGaugeLoss)` before `userStake` is loaded from storage. Since `userStake` is a return variable, `lastGaugeLoss` is initially 0. If `lastGaugeLoss` is > 0, `slashed` becomes true regardless of when the user staked.
preconditions: A term has experienced at least one gauge loss (`lastGaugeLoss > 0`)
A user has staked CREDIT in the SurplusGuildMinter for that term

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Evaluating condition against default-initialized return variable before loading state
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: `userStake.lastGaugeLoss` is 0 when the `if` condition is evaluated
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: State load `userStake = _stakes[user][term];` occurs after the check

analysis_questions: Does the compiler initialize return variables to zero?
Can an attacker call `getRewards` on behalf of other users?
validation_requirements: Write a test where a term has a loss, a user stakes afterward, and `getRewards` is called
Verify if the user's stake is zeroed out

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: An attacker monitors for any term that has had a loss. When users stake CREDIT in that term via SurplusGuildMinter, the attacker calls `getRewards(user, term)`. The bug causes `slashed` to be true, zeroing the user's stake and permanently locking their CREDIT in the ProfitManager.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Total loss of staked CREDIT for affected users
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All users staking on terms with historical losses
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: None, stake is zeroed and CREDIT is locked in ProfitManager

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests for staking after a gauge loss has occurred

technical_investigation_focus.execution_timing: Any time after a user stakes
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Easily detectable on-chain via Unstake events with 0 return

threats.title: DoS of Slashing and Token Transfers via Debt Ceiling Check
threats.hypothesis_description: The `_decrementGaugeWeight` function reverts if reducing the gauge weight causes the term's debt ceiling to fall below its current issuance, preventing slashing of bad debt and locking user tokens.
threats.investigation_rationale: When a term has bad debt, it is likely fully utilized. `applyGaugeLoss` calls `_decrementGaugeWeight` to slash users, which checks `issuance <= debtCeilingAfterDecrement`. If the term is fully utilized, this check fails, reverting the transaction. This protects voters from being slashed and prevents them from transferring their GUILD tokens.

proof_of_signal.pattern: State-dependent require in token transfer/burn path

preconditions: A lending term is utilized at or near its debt ceiling
A user attempts to transfer GUILD tokens or `applyGaugeLoss` is called

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Reverting on external state (`issuance`) during token transfer/burn
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Slashing (`applyGaugeLoss`) burns tokens, triggering this check
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No bypass for slashing or emergency weight reduction

analysis_questions: Does `applyGaugeLoss` always trigger `_decrementGaugeWeight`?
Can borrowers intentionally keep issuance high to prevent slashing?
validation_requirements: Simulate a fully utilized term with bad debt
Attempt to call `applyGaugeLoss` and observe the revert

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: A malicious borrower takes a loan up to the debt ceiling and intentionally defaults. When the protocol attempts to slash the GUILD voters who enabled this term via `applyGaugeLoss`, the transaction reverts because the debt ceiling would drop below the current issuance. Voters escape slashing, and their tokens are locked.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Protocol cannot slash bad actors; bad debt is not properly penalized
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Protocol (loss of slashing mechanism), GUILD holders (locked tokens)
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Requires borrowers to repay or loans to be fully auctioned

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests for slashing when a term is fully utilized

technical_investigation_focus.capital_requirements: Sufficient collateral to reach debt ceiling

threats.title: Infinite Loop in ERC20Gauges via Zero-Weight Gauge
threats.hypothesis_description: Adding a gauge with zero weight causes an infinite loop in `_decrementWeightUntilFree`, permanently bricking token transfers for the affected user.
threats.investigation_rationale: In `_decrementWeightUntilFree`, the loop variable `i` is only incremented inside the `if (userGaugeWeight != 0)` block. If a user has a gauge with 0 weight in their `_userGauges` set, the loop will evaluate it, skip the `if` block, and fail to increment `i`, causing an infinite loop and out-of-gas revert.
preconditions: User has a gauge in their `_userGauges` set with 0 weight
User attempts to transfer or burn tokens requiring weight to be freed

analysis_questions: Can a user add a gauge with 0 weight?
Can an attacker force a 0-weight gauge onto another user?
validation_requirements: Call `incrementGauge(gauge, 0)`
Attempt to transfer tokens and observe OOG revert

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: An attacker calls `incrementGauge(gauge, 0)` on their own account, then deposits their GUILD tokens into a DeFi protocol (e.g., a lending market). When the protocol attempts to liquidate the attacker by calling `transferFrom`, the transaction reverts with OOG, making the attacker unliquidatable.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Can be used to DoS smart contracts interacting with GUILD tokens
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Any user or contract holding GUILD
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: User must manually call `decrementGauge` to remove the 0-weight gauge

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests for transferring tokens after allocating 0 weight

threats.title: Deterministic Veto Proposal ID Prevents Retry
threats.hypothesis_description: The `createVeto` function generates a deterministic proposal ID based solely on the `timelockId`. If a veto fails, it cannot be retried, allowing attackers to intentionally fail vetoes early to protect malicious timelock actions.
threats.investigation_rationale: In `GuildVetoGovernor`, `createVeto` calls `super.propose` with deterministic arguments (target, value, calldata, description) derived from `timelockId`. OpenZeppelin's `Governor` hashes these to create the `proposalId`. If a proposal with this ID already exists (even if defeated), `propose` reverts. An attacker can create a veto immediately when an action is queued, knowing it will fail due to lack of votes, permanently preventing any future veto attempts.
preconditions: A timelock action is queued
An attacker has enough tokens to call `createVeto` (if any threshold exists)

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: OpenZeppelin Governor prevents duplicate proposal IDs
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No mechanism to retry a defeated veto

analysis_questions: Does `createVeto` have a proposal threshold?
Can the veto be executed before the voting period ends if it fails?
validation_requirements: Queue a timelock action
Call `createVeto` and let it fail
Attempt to call `createVeto` again and observe revert

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: A malicious governor queues a harmful action in the Timelock. Immediately, they (or an accomplice) call `createVeto`. Since honest users haven't voted yet, the veto fails. When honest users realize the danger and try to veto, `createVeto` reverts because the proposal ID already exists. The malicious action executes.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Complete bypass of the protocol's veto security mechanism

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests for retrying a failed veto

threats.title: Permanent PSM DoS via Repeated Offboarding
threats.hypothesis_description: Re-onboarding a term without cleaning it up allows permissionless re-offboarding, which permanently inflates the `nOffboardingsInProgress` counter and permanently pauses PSM redemptions.
threats.investigation_rationale: In `LendingTermOffboarding`, `offboard` increments `nOffboardingsInProgress` and pauses the PSM. `cleanup` decrements it and unpauses the PSM, while setting `canOffboard[term] = false`. If a term is offboarded, then re-onboarded by governance before `cleanup` is called, `canOffboard[term]` remains true. Anyone can call `offboard` again, incrementing the counter a second time. `cleanup` can only be called once (it sets `canOffboard` to false), leaving the counter permanently > 0.
preconditions: A term is offboarded
Governance re-onboards the term before `cleanup` is called

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: `canOffboard` is not reset on re-onboarding, allowing multiple `offboard` calls
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: `nOffboardingsInProgress` tracks global state but is manipulated per-term
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No check in `offboard` that the term isn't already contributing to the counter

analysis_questions: Can governance re-onboard a term while loans are active?
Does `GuildToken.addGauge` reset `canOffboard`? (No, it's a different contract)
validation_requirements: Offboard a term
Re-onboard it via `LendingTermOnboarding`
Call `offboard` again
Call `cleanup` and verify PSM remains paused

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: A term is legitimately offboarded. Governance decides to re-onboard it before all loans are closed (so `cleanup` hasn't been called). An attacker immediately calls `offboard(term)`. The counter increments to 2. Even after all loans close and `cleanup` is called, the counter drops to 1. PSM redemptions are permanently paused.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All CREDIT holders trying to redeem
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Requires governor intervention to manually unpause PSM or upgrade contracts

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests for re-onboarding before cleanup

threats.title: Profit Locked in ProfitManager for Zero-Weight Gauges
threats.hypothesis_description: If a lending term generates profit but its gauge weight is 0, the GUILD portion of the profit is permanently locked in the ProfitManager.
threats.investigation_rationale: In `notifyPnL`, `amountForGuild` is calculated based on `guildSplit`. If `_gaugeWeight != 0`, `gaugeProfitIndex` is updated. If `_gaugeWeight == 0`, the index is not updated, but `amountForGuild` is still subtracted from the total amount distributed to CREDIT holders. These CREDIT tokens remain in the ProfitManager balance with no accounting mechanism to claim them.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Conditional state update leaving funds unaccounted

preconditions: A lending term repays debt or settles an auction with profit
The gauge weight for that term is exactly 0

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Funds allocated but not distributed if condition fails
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: `amountForGuild` is not redirected to surplus buffer or CREDIT holders

analysis_questions: Can a gauge weight naturally reach 0 while loans are active?
Yes, if users un-vote or the term is offboarded and users remove weight.
validation_requirements: Set gauge weight to 0
Repay a loan with interest
Verify that `amountForGuild` CREDIT tokens are stuck in ProfitManager

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: A term is offboarded, and all users remove their gauge weight to avoid slashing. Borrowers continue to repay their loans, generating interest. The `guildSplit` portion of this interest is sent to ProfitManager but never added to any `gaugeProfitIndex`. The tokens are permanently locked, representing a yield leak for the protocol.
threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests for profit distribution to 0-weight gauges

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: ProfitManager balance exceeding accounted buffers and indices

threats.title: Perpetual Delay of Rebase Rewards via Frequent Distributions
threats.hypothesis_description: Anyone can perpetually delay the materialization of rebase rewards for all users by frequently calling `distribute()` with tiny amounts, resetting the 30-day interpolation period.
threats.investigation_rationale: In `ERC20RebaseDistributor`, calling `distribute(amount)` resets `__rebasingSharePrice.targetTimestamp` and `__unmintedRebaseRewards.targetTimestamp` to `block.timestamp + DISTRIBUTION_PERIOD` (30 days). If an attacker calls `distribute(1 wei)` every day, the interpolation period is constantly pushed back, meaning the previously distributed rewards will take infinitely long to fully vest.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Resetting interpolation end time on every update

preconditions: Protocol has pending unminted rebase rewards

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Overwriting `targetTimestamp` without accounting for remaining time of previous distributions
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: A 1 wei distribution delays millions of dollars in rewards
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No minimum distribution amount or weighted average time calculation

analysis_questions: What is the mathematical impact of resetting the period daily?
Does this practically prevent users from claiming their yield?
validation_requirements: Distribute a large amount of rewards
Advance time by 15 days
Distribute 1 wei
Verify that the remaining rewards now take 30 days instead of 15

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: A malicious actor wants to suppress the yield of CREDIT holders. They write a bot to call `distribute(1 wei)` every few hours. The 30-day interpolation period is constantly reset. CREDIT holders only receive a tiny fraction of their earned interest, effectively locking the yield in the unminted state indefinitely.
prerequisites: Attacker willing to spend gas and 1 wei of CREDIT

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Yield suppression for all rebasing users
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Stop the attacker or upgrade the contract

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests for the impact of frequent small distributions

threats.title: Strict minBorrow Check Prevents Exact Partial Repayments
threats.hypothesis_description: The `_partialRepay` function uses a strict greater-than (`>`) check against `minBorrow`, preventing borrowers from reducing their loan balance to exactly the minimum allowed amount.
threats.investigation_rationale: In `LendingTerm._partialRepay`, the check `require(borrowAmount - issuanceDecrease > ProfitManager(refs.profitManager).minBorrow(), "LendingTerm: below min borrow");` ensures the remaining loan size is valid. However, because it uses `>`, a borrower cannot leave exactly `minBorrow` debt. They must leave at least `minBorrow + 1`. This is a logic error that creates a small unusable range of debt.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Strict inequality used where inclusive inequality is expected

preconditions: A borrower attempts to partially repay their loan down to the minimum allowed size

analysis_questions: Does `borrow` allow exactly `minBorrow`? (Yes, `require(borrowAmount >= minBorrow)`)
Does this cause UX issues or block automated repayment strategies?
validation_requirements: Attempt to partially repay a loan leaving exactly `minBorrow`
Observe the revert

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: A borrower uses an automated strategy to maintain their loan size at the minimum allowed to maximize capital efficiency elsewhere. The strategy calculates the exact repayment needed to reach `minBorrow`. The transaction reverts, forcing the strategy to either fail or over-repay (fully closing the loan).
threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing boundary value tests for partial repayments

threats.title: ProfitManager notifyPnL Underflow Bricks Loss Realization
threats.hypothesis_description: If a loan's scaled principal exceeds the total CREDIT supply, `notifyPnL` underflows when calculating the new `creditMultiplier`, permanently preventing the auction from settling.
threats.investigation_rationale: In `notifyPnL`, if `loss >= surplusBuffer`, the new multiplier is `(creditMultiplier * (creditTotalSupply - loss)) / creditTotalSupply`. The `loss` is calculated based on `principal`, which is scaled up if `creditMultiplier` has dropped since the loan was opened (`borrowAmount * borrowCreditMultiplier / currentCreditMultiplier`). If `creditMultiplier` drops significantly, `principal` can exceed `creditTotalSupply`, causing an underflow.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Subtraction without checking if minuend >= subtrahend

preconditions: Protocol has suffered prior losses, reducing `creditMultiplier`
An old loan defaults where scaled principal > `creditTotalSupply`

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: `loss` can be artificially inflated by low `creditMultiplier`
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No floor on `creditMultiplier` or cap on `loss`

analysis_questions: How much must `creditMultiplier` drop for this to occur?
What happens to the collateral if `notifyPnL` reverts permanently?
validation_requirements: Simulate a scenario where `creditMultiplier` drops by 90%
Default a large old loan and attempt to settle the auction

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: The protocol suffers a series of bad debts, dropping `creditMultiplier` to 0.1e18. A whale who borrowed when the multiplier was 1e18 defaults. Their scaled principal is 10x their original borrow amount, exceeding the current total supply of CREDIT. The auction concludes, but `onBid` calls `notifyPnL`, which reverts due to underflow. The collateral is permanently locked.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Permanent locking of collateral and bad debt
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Borrowers and bidders involved in the auction

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests for extreme bad debt scenarios

threats.title: Rebase Exit Blocked by Share Price Interpolation Overflow
threats.hypothesis_description: When a large portion of the rebasing supply exits, the target share price recalculation can overflow `uint224`, permanently trapping the remaining rebasing users.
threats.investigation_rationale: In `updateTotalRebasingShares`, `targetNewSharePrice = currentRebasingSharePrice + (delta * START_REBASING_SHARE_PRICE) / percentChange`. If `sharesAfter` is very small compared to `sharesBefore`, `percentChange` is close to 0. This causes `targetNewSharePrice` to spike massively, potentially exceeding `type(uint224).max`. The subsequent `SafeCastLib.safeCastTo224(targetNewSharePrice)` will revert, blocking the transaction.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Division by small number causing overflow in downcast

preconditions: A distribution is currently interpolating (`delta > 0`)
A user holding a large majority of rebasing shares exits or transfers

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Inverse scaling of delta based on share reduction

analysis_questions: What is the exact ratio of `sharesAfter` to `sharesBefore` required to trigger the overflow?
Can an attacker intentionally create this state?
validation_requirements: Setup a state with 1 whale and 1 dust user in rebasing
Trigger a distribution
Have the whale exit rebasing and observe if it reverts

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: A whale holds 99.999% of rebasing shares. A distribution occurs. The whale calls `exitRebase()`. `sharesAfter` is 0.001% of `sharesBefore`. `percentChange` is tiny, causing `targetNewSharePrice` to exceed `uint224` max. The whale's transaction reverts. The whale is permanently trapped in rebasing, unable to transfer or exit.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Loss of access to funds for rebasing users
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Requires waiting for the distribution period to end (`delta == 0`)

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests for extreme share reduction during interpolation

technical_investigation_focus.capital_requirements: Large capital to dominate rebasing shares

threats.title: Governor forgive() Breaks Active Auctions
threats.hypothesis_description: If a governor calls `forgive()` on a loan that is currently in an active auction, the loan is closed but the auction remains open permanently, bricking the AuctionHouse migration process.
threats.investigation_rationale: In `LendingTerm`, `forgive()` sets `closeTime = block.timestamp` but does not check if `callTime != 0`. If the loan is in auction, `AuctionHouse` expects to call `onBid` to settle it. However, `onBid` requires `closeTime == 0`. The auction can never be settled, meaning `nAuctionsInProgress` in `AuctionHouse` will never decrement. This permanently blocks `setAuctionHouse`, which requires `nAuctionsInProgress == 0`.
preconditions: A loan is called and enters an active auction
Governor calls `forgive()` on that loan

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Missing state check for active auction

analysis_questions: Can the governor fix this using `emergencyAction`?
Does this affect other accounting metrics?
validation_requirements: Call a loan to start an auction
Call `forgive` as governor
Attempt to bid on the auction and observe revert
Attempt to call `setAuctionHouse` and observe revert

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: A loan enters an auction but collateral is frozen. The governor decides to step in and calls `forgive()` to realize the loss. The loan is closed, but the auction remains stuck in `AuctionHouse`. Later, when the DAO tries to upgrade the `AuctionHouse` via `setAuctionHouse`, the transaction reverts because `nAuctionsInProgress > 0`. The upgrade is permanently blocked.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Inability to upgrade core protocol infrastructure
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Requires complex `emergencyAction` to manually edit `AuctionHouse` storage

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests for `forgive` interaction with active auctions

threats.title: AuctionHouse bid() Reverts at Exact Auction End
threats.hypothesis_description: At the exact second an auction ends, `creditAsked` becomes 0, causing `bid()` to revert, forcing the loan to be forgiven as a total loss even if bidders are willing to take the collateral.
threats.investigation_rationale: In `AuctionHouse.getBidDetail`, if `block.timestamp >= _startTime + auctionDuration`, `creditAsked` becomes 0. The `bid()` function requires `creditAsked != 0`. Therefore, at the exact end of the auction, `bid()` reverts. The only way to close the auction is via `forgive()`, which marks the debt as a total loss (0 CREDIT recovered). This prevents bidders from taking the collateral for free/dust, which is the intended behavior of the Dutch auction's final stage.
preconditions: An auction reaches its maximum duration without being bid on

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Blocks legitimate bids at the end of the auction

analysis_questions: Is it intended that bidders cannot take collateral for 0 CREDIT?
If so, why does `getBidDetail` explicitly set `creditAsked = 0` (implicitly) in the final `else` block?
validation_requirements: Start an auction
Warp time to `startTime + auctionDuration`
Attempt to call `bid` and observe revert

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: An auction for a highly illiquid token reaches its final stage. A MEV bot waits until the last possible second to bid, aiming to get the collateral for 0 CREDIT. The transaction reverts because `creditAsked == 0`. The auction is stuck until someone calls `forgive()`, which burns the collateral in the lending term instead of distributing it to the market.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Collateral is locked in the lending term instead of being liquidated
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Protocol (loss of potential dust recovery), Bidders
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Collateral remains in LendingTerm, requires governance to extract

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests for bidding at the exact end of an auction
